Alternatively, if you're looking for a select number of Quant Strong Buy recommendations on a monthly basis, you might want to explore Alpha Picks.

data.title: EQT: Raising Target On Strong Free Cash Flow Upside
data.description:
I last profiled natural gas focused producer EQT Corp. (NYSE:EQT) in a May 8, 2024 piece here on Seeking Alpha titled “EQT: Cementing Position as Low-Cost Gas Producer.”

In that piece, I rated the stock a Strong Buy and set a target price of $56 per share based on a discounted cash flow model using free cash flow estimates over the period from 2024-2029.

Since that time, EQT has rallied 37.6%, outperforming the widely followed industry benchmark SPDR Oil & Gas Exploration and Production ETF's (XOP) 6% loss over a similar time frame by a more than 43 percentage point margin:

In addition to that, EQT touched my $56 target price this week after reporting solid Q4 2024 earnings results and issuing guidance for 2025.

In this article, I’ll examine EQT’s Q4 results compared to management’s guidance issued last October. I’ll also look at the company’s cost and production guidance for 2025 and evaluate what that means for the company’s potential free cash flow this year and next at current natural gas pricing.

As I’ll explain, based on the company’s strong operating performance, falling costs thanks to its acquisition of Equitrans last summer, and a more constructive environment for natural gas prices and related stocks, I’m also raising my target on EQT to $70 and maintaining my strong buy rating.

Q4 Results and Guidance Comparison

EQT Corp.’s third quarter results were well ahead of the midpoint of guidance presented on their Q3 earnings update back in late October.

Here’s a side-by-side comparison:

The left-hand column represents EQT’s guidance for Q4 results presented on their Q3 2024 conference call in late October. The right-hand column represents the actual results reported after the market close on February 18th.

As you can see, EQT beat across most line items.

Starting from the top, EQT’s total production came in at 605.2 billion cubic feet equivalent (Bcfe) for Q4 compared to the mid-point of guidance for 580 Bcfe. Indeed, at over 605 Bcfe, actual production was just above the top of the company’s guidance range issued back in late October.

There are many reasons a company can beat production guidance in a particular quarter and not all of those are good news. For example, if a company increased production by ramping up spending on drilling new wells, an increase in production could be offset by higher costs. Sometimes, a production “beat” is also a function of the timing of new wells coming online, which represents just quarter-to-quarter noise.

In this case, EQT’s management attributed the beat to “operational efficiency gains” and “strong well performance.”

That is good news because it means that EQT is producing more natural gas even with the same, or lower, capital spending.

That’s also backed up by the cost section of my side-by side comparison here.

For exploration and production (E&P) companies like EQT, capital spending (CAPEX) primarily represents money spent on drilling and completing (fracturing and putting into production) new wells. CAPEX also includes spending on midstream infrastructure such as the construction of new gas pipelines or gathering lines and processing facilities.

EQT offers guidance on CAPEX in three main buckets – maintenance CAPEX, “Strategic Growth” CAPEX and capital contributed to the company’s midstream joint venture called Equitrans. EQT acquired midstream company Equitrans last summer and then sold off a non-controlling interest in Equitrans to Blackstone (BX) back in November.

When EQT reports actual results, they report total CAPEX (including maintenance and strategic growth CAPEX) as well as capital contributed to Equitrans.

All in, the midpoint of EQT’s Q4 guidance was for $680 million in total CAPEX while the actual figure came in at $643.18 million. And what I really watch is the CAPEX per thousand cubic feet equivalent (Mcfe) of production; in this case, the combination of higher-than-expected production and lower than projected CAPEX was a CAPEX/mcfe of $1.06 compared to the $1.17 guidance.

That’s impressive, and it suggests real, sustainable capital efficiency gains for EQT rather than a one-quarter blip.

And when you consider that for EQT, just as for almost all the E&Ps in my coverage universe, CAPEX is the single largest cost line item, increasing capital efficiency is the key to generating free cash flow to perform shareholder-friendly moves such as paying down debt, paying dividends or buying back stock.

The next section of my table highlights production costs associated with EQT’s business including line items like gathering costs, which represents the cost of building out low diameter pipelines that connect individual wells to processing plants and long-distance pipelines. Transmission costs represent the cost of moving gas via long-distance pipelines while lease operating expenses (LOE) represent the cost of maintaining existing wells.

Also note that since EQT now controls Equitrans, the costs associated with functions like “Transmission” can also benefit EQT because it receives pipeline tariffs from third-party producers using midstream lines owned by Equitrans.

At any rate, all the production cost line items in my table are either in-line with or below the guidance EQT presented back in late October. Some of the positive variance in line items like gathering and transmission is likely due to better-than-expected cost savings and synergies resulting from the Equitrans acquisition last year, something EQT has discussed in recent conference calls.

The final section highlighted in red is the most important on my table as it represents EQT’s breakeven costs per Mcfe of gas produced. Using the all-in CAPEX figure, my estimates showed EQT’s Q4 guidance implied a free cash flow breakeven of $2.58/mcfe and the actual Q4 results showed an all-in breakeven of $2.43/mcfe.

If we look solely at CAPEX excluding Equitrans, the cash flow breakeven was guidance to $2.41/Mcfe and came in at $2.33/Mcfe.

Finally, if we look at the breakeven excluding Equitrans on an unlevered basis – excluding interest costs – EQT’s breakeven came in at $2.03 compared to the implied guidance of $2.14/Mcfe.

Of course, EQT must service its debt, so interest costs are important and the unlevered breakeven is less important than the all-in number in my view. However, in EQT’s case, I look at both figures, because the company assumed significant debt related to its purchase of Equitrans last year and is now paying down that debt at a rapid pace.

Specifically, EQT’s net debt – debt net of cash – stood at $13.7 billion as of the end of September last year, falling to $9.1 billion as of year-end. The company was able to pay down debt mainly due to the sale of its interest in producing wells that it doesn’t operate and the aforementioned midstream JV with Blackstone; these two deals netted proceeds of approximately $4.7 billion.

Importantly, the company previously guided to a net debt target of $7.5 billion by the end of 2025 and now sees debt ending this year at $7.0 billion with most of the debt paydown coming from free cash flow EQT is expected to generate through the current year (more on that in a moment).

So, in Q4, interest expenses were elevated compared to where they’re likely be in the current quarter and by the end of this year.

Let’s flip over and look at EQT’s guidance for 2025:

EQT Full-Year Guidance

Here are some estimates for EQT’s cost structure and production in 2025 based on the mid-point of the company’s guidance.

All these line items represent the midpoint of guidance except for interest cost for which management doesn’t offer guidance. Since the company has paid down about a third of its debt since the end of Q3 2024, as I explained earlier, I just penciled in a one-third reduction in the interest cost on a $/Mcfe basis.

For its full-year guidance, management offered additional granularity on their CAPEX.

Specifically, what I’ve labeled Maintenance CAPEX is upstream maintenance CAPEX – this is the money EQT would need to spend to drill, complete and put into production enough new wells to offset natural decline from existing wells. In other words, that’s roughly what EQT would need to spend to hold production constant.

Strategic Growth CAPEX is capital EQT spends to pave the way for future growth – this might include things such as building out new infrastructure or drilling wells in new acreage that would allow the company to grow output and free cash flow in future.

The midstream CAPEX number I’ve included in the table is midstream maintenance capital – money EQT needs to spend to maintain infrastructure and add enough new infrastructure—such as new gathering lines—to support the drilling and completion of new wells needed to maintain production.

The bottom line from all this is the section shaded red in the table above. On an all-in basis, the company’s free cash flow breakeven costs in 2025 are around $2.45/Mcfe and on a maintenance basis, it’s just $2.28/Mcfe. On an unlevered maintenance basis, EQT’s costs are down to around $2.08/Mcfe, which represents one of the lowest breakeven cost structures of any natural gas E&P I’ve examined including the names I’ve covered here on Seeking Alpha over the past two years.

The potential for free cash flow generation is impressive. For example, assuming EQT generates an all-in realized price of $4/mcfe for its production, the company would be on track to generate around $3.45 billion in free cash flow per year. That’s simply the difference between $4 and EQT’s all-in cost of $2.45/Mcfe multiplied by the midpoint of production guidance of 2,225 Bcfe.

Attractive Hedges

The company does have some hedges in place covering volumes produced in 2025:

This data is included in EQT’s Q4 earnings release and is updated each quarter.

I discussed at great length the importance of both the overall level of hedging and – arguably even more important – the way a company hedges production, in a recent piece here on Seeking Alpha regarding gas-focused producer CNX Resources (CNX).

In this case, the top rows of my table show the total volume of gas hedged by EQT each quarter through 2025 – this is both fixed price (swap) hedges as well as put options and options collars. To calculate the rough percent of production hedged line I simply assumed average quarterly production of 556.25 bcfe, which represents the midpoint of management’s guidance for the full year divided by four.

As you can see, EQT has hedged more than half of its production through the first three quarters of this year and most of those hedges are in the form of swaps. Swaps are fixed-price hedges – EQT has essentially locked in around half of its production for the first 9 months of 2025 at prices around $3.11/MMbtu to $3.49/MMBtu.

As I'll show you in just a moment, that's below the current level of gas prices in the NYMEX futures market.

However, look at Q4. While the total amount EQT hedged for Q4 is still a hefty 51% of total production, two-thirds of those hedges are in the form of put options NOT swaps. Put options lock in a “floor” price for EQT’s production – should natural gas prices fall below $3.30/MMBtu in Q4 of this year then these puts would protect EQT’s cash flow from that weakness. However, these puts do not limit EQT’s upside if prices are much higher than $3.30/MMBtu.

It's also worth looking at the short call option line on my table. By selling call options, EQT generates premium to offset the cost of buying put options; however, there is still a cost in that EQT is essentially giving away commodity price upside above the strike price of those calls.

In this case, however, EQT has only sold significant calls covering production in Q1 and Q4 of this year and for both quarters the strike prices of those calls are high at $4.19 and $5.49/MMbtu respectively.

Keep this table in mind and examine the futures curve for NYMEX-traded natural gas right now:

I sourced this data directly from the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) website right here.

Natural gas futures are traded with expirations in every month of the year and the orange line in my chart shows the current trading price for natural gas for delivery in every month from March 2025 (the current front month) through March 2026.

The blue line represents what’s known as the calendar strip for each quarter from Q2 2025 through Q1 2026 – this is the average price of gas futures for each quarter. For example, the calendar strip for Q3 2025 is the average price of the July, August and September 2025 futures, which currently trades around $4.34/MMBtu.

The important thing to notice is that US-traded natural gas futures have a distinct upward slope – a condition known as contango – in that the calendar strip for Q2 2025 is $4.12/MMBtu, rising to $4.66/MMBtu in Q1 2026.

Thus, the ideal scenario is for a producer to have significant unhedged production -- or production that’s not hedged using fixed price swaps – for late 2025 and early 2026 when the natural gas calendar strips are trading at higher prices.

That describes EQT’s current situation well – the company has limited fixed price hedges in place in Q4 of 2025 and no hedges for Q1 2026.

This positions EQT well to benefit from the attractive strip prices late this year into early 2026.

All told, in the Q4 2024 earnings release earlier this week, EQT estimates that based on the current hedges in place, its cost guidance and the calendar strip for gas in 2025 and 2026, it can generate $2.6 billion in free cash flow this year and $3.3 billion in free cash flow next year.

That’s in line with the estimates I derived above at a flat gas price of $4/MMBtu based on the cost and CAPEX guidelines mentioned earlier.

I’d note two points.

First, EQT has tended to under-promise and overdeliver on cost synergies related to its Equitrans deal last year – I suspect they’re being conservative when it comes to their 2025 guidance and they may ultimately see lower costs, more production and more free cash flow than implied by the midpoint of their guidance I just outlined. That’s exactly what they delivered in Q4 2024 as I explained.

Second, the big issue facing most producers in Appalachia is the lack of pipeline takeaway capacity – there aren’t enough pipelines to move natural gas out of the region to areas where gas prices are higher such as the US Gulf Coast or New England.

The last major pipeline to be built out of the region and put into service was the Mountain Valley Pipeline (MVP), in which EQT now owns a majority interest and acts as operator following the Equitrans deal. So, while most producers in the region struggle to grow production, owning the midstream infrastructure gives EQT more flexibility to grow gas output over time if prices are attractive, leveraging its preferential access to takeaway capacity.

So, I suspect that in future years EQT will be in a position to grow gas production well above the mid-point of this year's guidance at 2,225 bcfe.

For example, at 2,400 bcfe (annualized run rate of their Q4 2023 output), an all-in production cost of $2.40/mcfe and a natural gas strip price of $4.25/MMBtu, EQT would generate close to $4.5 billion in annualized free cash flow. That’s not a pie-in-the-sky outlook given their current cost structure, deleveraging goals and modest upside to cost and efficiency gains related to the Equitrans deal.

Conclusion, Target and Risks

In my last bullish piece on EQT last May I used a discounted cash flow model to derive a conservative valuation target for EQT of $56 per share. However, last May natural gas, and natural gas producers, were decidedly out of favor with investors, so valuation multiples were constrained.

In a $4.00 or $4.50/MMBtu environment with some volume growth potential and downside to costs, I believe EQT can trade at a roughly 8% free cash flow yield based on its enterprise value. Assuming upside to $4 billion in annualized free cash flow as soon as 2026, an 8% yield points to upside to an enterprise value – value of the stock and outstanding debt – of $50 billion.

Assuming EQT meets its year-end 2025 debt target of $7 billion that points to a $43 billion market cap (enterprise value less net debt), approximately 35% above the current quote or about $70 per share. That’s my new, raised target for EQT.

As with all commodity producers, the biggest downside risk for EQT is lower-than-expected commodity prices. Of course, that would deflate the value of EQT based on discounted cash flow models such as I outlined in my piece last May.

In addition, in a depressed natural gas pricing environment such as we saw through much of 2024, natural gas E&Ps tend to trade at depressed valuation multiples to financial metrics like free cash flow and EBITDA. My new, higher multiple assumed a transition to average gas pricing closer to $4/MMBTu in future compared to $2.00 to $2.50 last year. When EQT is generating real free cash flow, I believe investors will get more aggressive valuing the stock and EQT can support a higher multiple.

Commodity risks remain, but I rate EQT a strong buy with a target price of $70.

data.title: Artisan Global Discovery Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investing Environment

US equities posted solid gains in Q4, capping off a robust year. After Donald Trump’s presidential election victory, investors cheered the prospect of deregulation, corporate tax cuts and the reshoring of manufacturing benefiting the economy and, ultimately, the stock market. This optimism faded in December as concerns about potential inflationary pressures from proposed tariffs and strict immigration policies led to hawkish Fed speak and reintroduced downside volatility to the market. Still, markets finished 2024 with solid gains, capping off one of the best two-year performance stretches in decades.

The Republican sweep in November, while causing a positive shift in sentiment for risk assets, also provoked concerns about inflation. These concerns led to a US Treasury market selloff, with yields up across the curve. The 2-year, 10-year and 30-year Treasurys were up 63bps, 83bps and 70bps, respectively. Amid the selloff, the Fed held meetings in November and December, cutting the fed funds rate by 25bps at each meeting. Despite these cuts coming on the heels of a 50bps cut in September, the expectation of future rate cuts shifted dramatically.

Outside the US, eurozone equities retreated in Q4 as recession fears and political instability in France and Germany weighed on sentiment. Concerns over potential trade wars following Trump’s electoral win further dampened investor confidence. The European Central Bank (ECB) responded to weak growth by cutting interest rates by 25bps in both October and December. ECB President Christine Lagarde signaled further cuts in 2025, emphasizing a more accommodative monetary policy to counter sluggish economic growth. Emerging markets equities also faced headwinds in Q4, driven by Trump’s electoral win and the prospect of new tariffs, particularly targeting China.

Following the trend from much of 2023 and 2024, markets in Q4 were led by US mega cap and more growth-oriented equities. Looking at the MSCI ACWI Index, sector performance was led by consumer discretionary, communication services and information technology, with several Magnificent Seven stocks contributing to the rally. In contrast, the materials and health care sectors lagged.

Exhibit 1: Index Returns

Performance Discussion

The portfolio generated strong absolute returns in Q4 and 2024, and it outperformed the MSCI AC World Small Mid Index over each period. Q4 performance was driven by strong security selection, where portfolio holdings outperformed in 9 of the 10 sectors we invested in. While health care was one of the worst performing sectors for the index in Q4, our portfolio generated strong relative results due to strong execution across multiple biotechnology and pharmaceutical holdings, including Argenx (ARGX), Veracyte (VCYT) and Galderma (OTCPK:GDERF). Communication services outperformed due to rallies in our Internet and media holdings, including Spotify (SPOT), Liberty Media Formula One, Live Nation (LYV) and Sea, driven by robust earnings and positive forward outlooks. And in information technology, software industry holdings Atlassian and HubSpot improved significantly, as easing macroeconomic pressures and early adoption of artificial intelligence (AI)-enhanced features bolstered earnings performance.

Among our top Q4 contributors were MACOM (MTSI) and Galderma. MACOM Technology Solutions designs and manufactures high-performance semiconductors. The company’s relatively new management team is taking steps to accelerate top-line growth and expand margins by addressing smaller, long-duration product cycle markets in which it can provide a differentiated offering, especially in compound semis (those made from two or more elements). Shares have outperformed due to steady growth of data center and defense end markets. As a member of the US Department of Defense’s trusted foundry program, MACOM is a trusted manufacturer of US military and aerospace applications and offers a comprehensive portfolio of products that support the demanding performance requirements of today’s aerospace and defense systems. Meanwhile, we believe the more cyclical areas of the business within industrial and telecommunications are not far from a recovery phase that will provide meaningful earnings upside in the years to come. After the period of strong performance, we trimmed the position.

Galderma is a science-based dermatology business. The company is a leader in injectable aesthetics (neuromodulators, fillers and biostimulators), owns the Cetaphil skincare franchise (a brand that is expected to do more than $1 billion in revenue this year) and has a leading portfolio of drugs for acne/rosacea. We expect the global dermatology market to continue to grow at attractive levels and the company to outgrow the overall market by leveraging its scale, breadth and innovation in aesthetics while meaningfully expanding its margin profile. Shares outperformed following earnings results, where management reaffirmed its forward guidance of 7%–10% growth with strong EBITDA margins.

Among our top detractors were Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) and Gerresheimer (OTCPK:GRRMF). Monolithic Power Systems designs analog power-management chips for a wide variety of industrial and consumer devices. Shares declined after the company reported strong growth in its data center segment that trailed investors’ lofty expectations, which had driven the stock up YTD. Also, while the segment is expected to grow in 2025, the company will face some temporary headwinds as additional power semiconductor suppliers are expected to be qualified to supply NVIDIA’s (NVDA) graphical processing unit (GPU) needs. While we’re cognizant of this headwind in 2025, we expect it to be manageable and believe the company has other meaningful growth drivers in store for the year (particularly in autos). Our valuation discipline had led us to reduce the position earlier in the year, but given a more reasonable starting point in Q4, we decided to add to the position.

Gerresheimer develops and produces specialty products made of glass and plastic for pharmaceutical companies. After years of lackluster growth, we believe the new management team will deliver on accelerating revenue growth as it focuses on higher value products (ready-to-fill vials, higher resistance glass vials, smart devices) and on biologics. Furthermore, recent capex investments should remove capacity bottlenecks and drive increased automation, which, combined with a positive mix shift toward higher value products, should drive EBITDA margins higher. Shares have struggled throughout the year as it grappled with elevated customer inventories of packaging components purchased to derisk COVID-related supply chain constraints, ultimately resulting in a lower 2024 guidance. Furthermore, a key facility in North Carolina was impacted by Hurricane Helene. However, we believe the company is nearing an inflection point as these headwinds ease. Meanwhile, the company remains well positioned to benefit from the growth in GLP-1 obesity drugs, in our view, where it has been working with Novo Nordisk on dual-chamber syringes.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated 10 new Garden SM positions, including Pure Storage (PSTG) and The Sage Group (OTCPK:SGGEF). Pure Storage is redefining enterprise data storage by bypassing traditional approaches used by competitors. Instead of purchasing costly enterprise-grade solid-state drives (SSDs) and layering software on top, Pure Storage sources raw NAND flash memory and designs custom all-flash storage systems, providing a cost advantage. The company has been gaining share of the core enterprise data storage market due to this offering, but we believe its recent announcement of securing a large hyperscaler data center contract potentially opens the company up to a much larger addressable market and gives us confidence that the company can accelerate its growth trajectory over the next two to three years.

Through decades of organic growth and strategic M&A, Sage has established itself as a leading software provider for small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) across Europe and the US. The company’s solutions streamline back-office operations, including enterprise resource planning (ERP), financials, HR and payroll. We anticipate accelerating revenue growth driven by a product mix shift toward faster growing offerings, international expansion, cross-selling opportunities and pricing increases across the existing customer base. Additionally, we expect margin expansion as Sage continues to migrate customers from on-premises solutions to the cloud. This transition should enhance functionality, integrate AI innovations and position Sage to deliver greater value to its clients at higher margins.

Along with Monolithic Power Systems, a notable add in the quarter was Novonesis (OTCPK:NVZMF). Novonesis was born out of the merger between Novozyme and Chr Hansen, which created a global biosolutions powerhouse that aimed to transform consumer products and make industrial and agricultural processes more sustainable and efficient. The company has an unrivaled portfolio breadth and an industry-leading R&D spend that should support continued innovation. We view the company as a sustainability enabler, given most of its products either directly support healthy lives and well-being or are substitutes for traditional petrochemical-based ingredients. We believe the company will experience accelerating growth due to successful innovations and better commercialization execution led by a relatively new CEO. Furthermore, we believe the company’s cost synergy expectations from the merger are conservative, implying potential margin upside. Earnings results were thesis affirming, including organic growth that exceeded expectations, driven by strong momentum across each business segment.

We ended our investment campaigns in Arista Networks (ANET) and Monday.com (MNDY) during the quarter. Arista Networks is the market leader in cloud networking equipment used in data centers. Shares have strongly outperformed since the beginning of 2023 as its ethernet options capture market share in AI cloud environments. Many of the largest buyers in this space are focused on utilizing Arista’s networking technology, given meaningful increases in GPU utilization rates versus InfiniBand, the out-of-the-box solution from NVIDIA. We believe Arista remains exceptionally well positioned. However, after a successful multiyear campaign, we sold the position due to valuation and an elevated market cap.

Monday.com is a software provider specializing in project management offerings for businesses of all sizes. Our thesis was based on the company’s core project management product being differentiated due to its ease of implementation and wide use cases, driving attractive payback periods of two years. Meanwhile, the company was introducing new products for new and existing customers, and the management team was pivoting toward a more disciplined expense structure. The stock was a strong performer, but in the recent quarterly results, which were solid overall, it displayed signs of slowing growth. Given the stock’s elevated valuation, we no longer see the risk-reward as attractive relative to other opportunities.

Along with MACOM, a notable trim in the quarter included ON Semiconductor. ON Semiconductor is a leading designer and manufacturer of chips for power management and image sensing. From a battery-electric vehicle standpoint, ON is a leading producer of silicon carbide chips. Shares have been under pressure as the company grapples with multiple quarters of inventory right-sizing across the auto supply chain and slower-than-expected EV sales growth. While ON is seeing smaller sales declines than peers due to its market share gains, we are concerned that moderating US and European EV growth trends will weigh on the company’s 2025 performance, which led us to trim the position.

Stewardship Update

The growth in data centers has significantly increased demand for reliable, large-scale and carbon-free energy sources. Data centers are energy-intensive, requiring vast amounts of electricity to power servers, cooling systems and infrastructure. With the expansion of AI, energy consumption by data centers is projected to grow exponentially. By some estimates, data centers could account for up to 8%–10% of global electricity consumption by 2030, prompting the need for sustainable and continuous energy sources.

Meanwhile, technology giants like Google, Microsoft and Amazon have committed to reducing their carbon footprints and achieving net-zero emissions. While wind and solar are crucial for clean energy transitions, their intermittent nature creates challenges for data centers, which require 24/7 power. Given this conflict of accelerating power needs versus carbon reduction commitments, our team has focused on the implications.

One option for these hyperscalers may be to pursue nuclear power sources, a low-carbon energy source that provides a stable energy supply to ensure uninterrupted operations. For example, Constellation Energy announced a 20-year deal with Microsoft that will reopen the Three Mile Island nuclear plant in Pennsylvania to supply power to an AI data center. Interest in the prospect of small modular reactors (SMRs) is also increasing. Advocates for SMRs believe their smaller size and faster deployment timelines make them an attractive solution. However, our optimism in this area is tempered by the relatively immature state of the technology and the stated hesitancy of regulated utilities to invest in anything nuclear.

Based on our research today, we believe that hyperscalers will pursue clean energy power sources wherever possible to satisfy their power needs. However, the evidence is mounting that they may need to push their emissions goals if they are going to satisfy energy demand. This will likely have investment implications for the energy industry, particularly for companies exposed to natural gas, which may become the go-to fuel source, especially in areas where renewable energy infrastructure is not yet robust or reliable enough to ensure consistent power delivery.

Perspective

Global equities, as reflected by the MSCI AC World Index, have delivered a very strong two-year performance run. We are cognizant of the expansion in valuation multiples that has accompanied this rally, as well as the extreme momentum experienced by several “hot” growth stocks at the top of the index (which is further evidence of robust animal spirits in the market today, and we believe has distorted the index performance as discussed earlier).

Looking past these momentum leaders, we find the portfolio's valuations to be more reasonable, albeit not cheap. We have been trimming some highly valued securities in favor of more opportunistic investments. Fundamentally, recent earnings reports for portfolio holdings have generally been quite solid, and our conviction in the profit cycles driving the portfolio seems well supported entering 2025.

In particular, our team’s Q4 research and travel strengthened our confidence in many of the profit cycles driving our IT holdings. We own multiple category leaders within technology that are gaining market share partly due to how well positioned they are to help enable AI advances. Examples include MACOM (networking chips), Pure Storage (data storage) and Onto Innovation (inspection systems for advanced chips). In addition, our cloud software franchises are making steady progress in introducing AI functionality into their leading solution suites. Meanwhile, strong profit cycle momentum from our Internet and media holdings (such as Spotify, Liberty Media Formula One, Live Nation and Sea) look poised to continue in 2025 as these franchises benefit from strong competitive positions and a solid mix of revenue and profitability growth.

Health care, by contrast, has been largely left behind in this bull market and now presents some of our portfolio's most attractive valuation opportunities. For example, we anticipate profit acceleration this year for our biotech holdings (Argenx, Ascendis and Insmed) as they launch new medicines with blockbuster sales potential. Inventory headwinds in the biologic drug packaging sector should also clear, enabling West Pharmaceuticals, Gerresheimer and Sartorius to resume their compelling long-term growth trajectory.

In addition to valuations, we enter the new year with an increased focus on domestic and international political uncertainty. Most significantly, the incoming Trump administration is expected to propose specific (and likely aggressive) policy changes across trade, labor, government spending, regulation and health care. We're paying close attention to how these changes do and don't get enacted, and we are integrating them into our assessments of investment risks and rewards. The signal-to-noise ratio is expected to be high, and we'll lean on our team of sector experts to help us tell the difference. Of course, these policy changes will likely also impact interest rates, which could continue to be a source of market volatility ahead.

data.title: Wix.com: Bookings Acceleration Highlights This Tremendous Value Play
data.description:
With the stock market finding new all-time highs in early 2025, investors who are cautious on valuations should rotate more and more of their portfolios toward "growth at a reasonable price" stocks. These may not be the flashiest growth stocks available on the market, but they are offering tremendous fundamentals without bandwagon pricing.

Wix.com (NASDAQ:WIX), the website hosting company, is a great buy in this regard, especially after the company's modest post-Q4 earnings drop. Despite a significant beat to Wall Street's earnings expectations and a meaningful uplift in gross bookings rates (which is a strong indicator for revenue growth in FY25), shares of Wix slid ~5% after the print. We shouldn't be shedding tears for Wix, whose stock has been on a tear and is up ~70% over the past year: however, in my view, this rise has been fully supported by fundamentals, and the post-earnings dip offers a great opportunity to buy Wix on the pause.

I last wrote a bullish note on Wix in December, when the stock was trading above $220 per share. Since then, Wix's bookings trends have improved, while the company issued a robust FY25 outlook that indicates a continuation of strong growth rates alongside a meaningful accumulation of FCF - and as such, I'm reiterating my buy opinion on Wix.

It's important for long-term investors to recognize that Wix is in the midst of two meaningful business transitions. The first is that Wix is shifting away from targeting any and every customer. It hiked its pricing in 2024 and is deliberately focusing on larger customers that it believes are "higher intent." In addition, the company is leveraging the sales forces of more prominent reseller partners, which is helping Wix to land larger deals. Wix's 6.2 million premium subscribers as of the end of FY24 shed 72k total paid subscribers in the year - but its install base is generating double-digit bookings growth, alongside a 106% net revenue retention rate (indicating a 6% average upsell rate).

The second major transition is, naturally, AI. The company has rolled out a slew of interesting new features in this space. In January, the company rolled out a feature called Business Launcher. This unique product gives customers the ability to chat with an AI agent, which gives customers ideas for launching a new business and lays out a step-by-step guide to get started.

All in all, Wix has a number of catalysts to sustain its excellent growth trajectory, alongside a consistent trend for improving its margin performance. Stay long here and use the post-earnings dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Wix's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The company's latest quarterly results are shown below:

Wix's revenue grew 14% y/y to $460.5 million, essentially in-line with Wall Street's expectations of $461.4 million while also accelerating slightly from 13% y/y growth in Q3.

Where I continue to focus my attention, however, is on Wix's pace of bookings growth - as it's ultimately the company's bookings that will get recognized as revenue in future quarters. Wix notched $465 million of net-new bookings in Q4, growing at an 18% y/y pace - which accelerated from 16% y/y in Q3 and 15% y/y in Q2.

To me, Wix's consistent trend of accelerating bookings is a validation of its strategy to pursue higher-value enterprise accounts. Several drivers are helping Wix to achieve faster bookings growth. The first is a broader network of top-tier reseller partners. The company notes that deals closed by partners increased at a 30% y/y pace.

Features are also another important avenue for Wix's customer acquisition. Alongside the potential for the newly-launched Business Launcher, the company notes that its AI Website Builder feature has already been used to publish more than 1 million websites.

The company's move upmarket as well as its pricing bumps are also helping to drive both revenue and bookings growth. Per CEO Nir Zohar's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Additionally, average bookings per subscription or ABPS increased 13% year-over-year to more than $294. This step up in monetization was driven by the continued shift to higher tiered packages, increased adoption of business solutions and compounding GPV as we onboarded and retained high intent users such as commerce oriented users and more upmarket partners. ABPS also benefited from the price increase implemented earlier this year which was absorbed better than historical increases, another testament to the improving quality of our user base over recent years [...]

Existing cohort behavior improved compared to the prior year, demonstrated by net revenue retention increasing to 106% in 2024. We expect our growing product offering and continued ability to attract partners and larger commerce users will increase revenue retention going forward. Our initiatives in recent years have undoubtedly shifted our base in favor of higher intent users such as Partners and commerce users. This dynamic has been meaningfully accretive to our business, as demonstrated by the strong top line growth achieved in 2024. We expect this positive momentum to continue into 2025 as we drive our strategic initiatives forward and innovate to add new layers of long term growth.

At the same time as Wix is driving meaningful top-line growth, the company is also delivering impressive operating leverage. As shown in the chart below, the company managed to bring opex as a percentage of revenue down 6 points y/y to 48%, with plans for 2-3 points of further operating leverage in FY25:

Wix is also a burgeoning cash flow story. In FY24, cash flow nearly doubled, up 98% y/y to $488 million and representing a 28% FCF margin - 12 points better y/y. The company expects FCF margins to gain an additional 2-3 points next year, with overall cash flow growing ~23% at the midpoint to a range of $590-$610 million:

Wix measures its Rule of 40 as revenue growth plus FCF margins (whereas most other companies use adjusted EBITDA margins or adjusted operating margins), which I view to be a stricter measure than how most of its peers report it (as FCF conversion tends to be lower than adjusted EBITDA or adjusted operating profits). We note that by its own measure, Wix achieved a Rule of 40 score of 41 this year, and it's managing the business to hit at least 45 next year - a sign of incredible balance between growth and profitability.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $217, Wix trades at a market cap of $11.90 billion. After we net off the $773.8 million of cash and $572.9 million of debt on Wix's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $11.70 billion.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year FY25, the company has guided to 12-14% y/y growth in revenue to $1.97-$2.00 billion (with a stronger U.S. dollar acting as a two-point headwind to growth), alongside 23% y/y growth in FCF as previously mentioned to a midpoint of $600 million.

This puts Wix's valuation multiples at:

  • 5.9x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 19.5x EV/FY25 FCF

We note that Wix trades cheaper than other software companies like Workday (WDAY) and Salesforce (CRM) whose growth is also clocking in the low/mid-teens. In addition, Wix is only trading a hair richer than its direct competitor GoDaddy (GDDY), despite the fact that GoDaddy's revenue growth has trailed to just the high single digits.

My price target on Wix is $257, representing 7x EV/FY25 revenue and ~18% upside from current levels. Stay long here and use the post-earnings dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Waters Corporation: Instrument Replacement Cycle Momentum, But Valuation Is A Concern
data.description:
Introduction

Waters Corporation (NYSE:WAT) reported their Q4’24 and FY’24 results on February 12 and the results came in better than expected. Over the last few quarters, the company has been coming out of a downturn in instruments that have been longer and deeper than prior cycles. In the latest results, we’ve seen the recovery is starting to pick up with strength led by the Academics and Government segment and pharma showing continued momentum. In this piece, I’ll analyze the most recent quarterly results and provide my thoughts on the company’s near-term and long-term outlook, as well as how I’m thinking about valuation today.

Gross margin hiccup, but FX to blame

Waters Corp closed out 2024 with a strong quarter that beat on both the top and bottom line. During the quarter, the company delivered revenue growth of 6.4% compared to last year with sales clocking in at $872.7 million, beating estimates by $15.9 million. That said, coming into the quarter, the company had a tough setup going in as investors likely expected a strong Q4 given strong instrumentation growth from rest of peers and for management to guide FY’25 conservatively at mid-single digit growth. And even though management delivered on these expectations, the gross margins seemed to have weighed on the stock-post quarter.

In terms of margins, gross margins came in at 60.0% compared to last year’s 61.2% which is obviously not something you want to see for a business that supposedly is high margin with pricing power. On the company’s earnings call, management discussed how foreign exchange headwinds were about 170bps, implying close to 100% drop-through, which is well above what we have seen in the past and what they have discussed prior. Given the strength of the U.S. dollar in recent months, the typical offsets/hedges were probably not enough to offset the velocity in the rate changes that the company saw in the quarter and likely explains some of the elevated impact.

As for the other expense line items, SG&A as a percentage of revenues decreased, as did R&D and net interest expense. Notwithstanding the gross margin impact from FX then, I’d say that this was a good quarter in my view, especially with EPS of $4.10 up 13% year over year and beating Wall Street estimates by 7 cents in spite of it all.

Pacing of instrument replacement cycle is key

As for what drove results this quarter, in Q4’24, Waters Corp characterized the budget flush as “typical” (meaning stronger than the last 2 years but trending toward a normalized level) and noted it saw a high-teens ramp sequentially in the quarter, above expectations of mid-teens growth, driven by the large pharma instrument replacement cycle beginning and a budget flush benefit in Europe, which grew 11% overall including 20% in academic in the region. On the earnings call management pointed out that historically, when coming out of a trough in instrument replacement, Waters Corp has usually seen 2-3% outperformance long-term instrument growth average of 5%.

For the growth drivers of Waters Corp’s business, revenues this quarter have been driven by 10% organic growth in Pharma, a 2% increase in Industrial, and 16% growth in Academia and Government driven by the strength in markets like Asia and Europe. One of the things that stood out to me on the earnings call was that the company emphasized that spending by large pharma, CDMOs, and generics customers is continuing to recover.

By product group, instrument revenues grew 8% organically while recurring revenues like chemistry and service revenues 9% organically, split between 7% growth in chemistry and 9% growth in service. In terms of specific products, the Alliance IS represented approximately 20% of the company’s HPLC revenues in Q4’24 as its adoption increased throughout the year. In MS, sales of the Xevo TQ Absolute system grew by 40% year over year, contributing around half of tandem quad sales. While these are smaller parts of Waters Corp’s business, they do highlight some of the growth drivers within their portfolio.

Meanwhile, in the chemistry consumables segment, Max Premier columns sales increased by 30% in the quarter, with 40% growth for the year. By geography, Americas grew 6% organically with 11% organic growth in Europe, and 9% growth in Asia. This shows that growth isn’t really limited to one region, and that demand across all markets is generally healthy.

Outlook

Looking ahead, Waters Corp is focused on executing in core business lines as well as high-growth areas such as GLP-1 testing, PFAS testing, and the generics market in India which is expected to add 70bps to 100bps. In my view, with India growing 34% driven by the expanding generics market, I expect continued market leadership by Waters Corp. Management has noted that they see significant opportunities in the generics market with some $240 billion of revenues expected to go off-patent in the next 5-7 years. With India currently supplying 50% of the global generics market, I think there’s a lot of opportunities for continued growth in the medium term.

For 2025, Waters Corp anticipates continued strong momentum, with growth projected between 4.5% and 7%. Despite the strengthening U.S. dollar, which is expected to create foreign exchange headwinds, the company plans to counterbalance these impacts through robust operational performance sustaining its margin expansion trends into 2025. This translates to a full-year earnings per fully diluted share guidance of $12.70 to $13 which implies 7% to 10% growth compared to last year.

In my view, the guidance seems achievable. Management is expecting recurring revenue growth of 6-7%, with instrument growth around 4.5%. To me, this looks realistic given that recurring revenue for WAT has consistently grown in this range, even through different macroeconomic cycles so it’s reassuring that they’re confident in this trend continuing.

As for instruments, the company expects steady growth across key areas like mass spectrometry, liquid chromatography, and thermal analysis. From what I can tell, the optimism about 2025 seems justified, especially with the replacement cycle in large pharma kicking off and new products like Alliance in HPLC and Xevo TQ in mass spectrometry gaining traction. Plus, there are a few emerging growth drivers, like PFAS and generics in India, that could add some extra momentum.

One thing that stood out to me was that pricing is also expected to continue supporting growth, which, I think, makes sense given how consistently it’s contributed in recent years. When we look at the breakdown by end-market, pharma is projected to grow around 7%, which is realistic considering the sector’s stability, with Industrial growing at a slower pace around 5%. The academic/government sector is a bit more uncertain, especially with a dip expected (guide from management) in Q1’25, but I think management’s cautious approach here makes sense given the potential for shifts in funding.

Risks and valuation

In terms of the risks to the investment thesis, I think the biggest risk is a lack of meaningful opportunities to deploy cash. Multiples in the space are elevated and buybacks make less sense with valuations close to all-time highs. In my view, this risk supersedes the concern around the pace and sustainability of the analytical instrument market's recovery. Long-term, I’m pretty sure this can recover, and management seems optimistic about potential instrument replacement cycle driving growth but with a slow rebound in customer capex spending. Still, the company is still reliant on the timing of this cycle and much of the risk comes down to the price investors are paying versus the value they are getting.

On valuation, I find it hard to justify the current price given the growth ahead. When looking at sell side estimates for the stock, most analysts see high-single digit to low-double digit earnings growth going forward, mostly driven by multiple expansion and share buybacks as the top line grows around 5% per year.

However, when we consider that investors today are paying 29.0x earnings or 21.0x EV/EBITDA for a medium growth company, I think shares are likely fairly priced at best (source: Bloomberg). Indeed, Waters Corp trades at premiums of 20% and 17%, respectively, relative to the company’s long-term five-year average. It also trades at a premium to the peer group average that trades at 24.2x P/E and 18.0x EV/EBITDA.

All in all, I think Waters Corp’s latest results highlight a solid recovery driven by growth in key sectors like academia, government, and pharma. Despite a slight dip in gross margins due to foreign exchange headwinds, the company delivered strong performance with healthy growth in both instrument and recurring revenue categories. Looking ahead, I’m optimistic about the company’s exposure to emerging markets such as India and in high-growth product areas like GLP-1 and PFAS testing. However, I do find the company’s current valuation somewhat challenging, as it appears to be priced at a premium compared to its historical averages and peers. So while I remain confident in the company’s long-term growth potential, I think it’s important for investors to balance this with the price they’re paying today. For now, I rate the stock as a ‘hold’.

data.title: Rates Spark: Eurozone PMIs Will Test Positive Macro Sentiment
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Benjamin Schroeder

PMIs have less influence over the front end than before

Data surprises have turned more positive for the eurozone recently, and the round of PMIs today will be the next test. The last reading of the eurozone PMI composite index edged above the 50 breakeven level again, thanks to an uptick in manufacturing. But overall, the manufacturing industry continues to show signs of weakness, with the German index in contractionary territory for over two years now.

Markets seem in good spirits in terms of the growth outlook and would not be happy to see a turn lower in PMIs. Equities have shown an exceptionally strong performance this year, and credit spreads have tightened further. Yet, we shouldn’t forget how quickly sentiment can turn. November last year saw rates significantly below today on the back of mounting growth concerns, both in the US and the eurozone. One round of weak PMIs was enough to justify an additional rate cut by the European Central Bank in October.

Since the lows in November, the ECB has cut the policy rate by another 50bp, limiting the potential impact from PMIs on the front end of the curve. Recent hawkish comments by the ECB have pushed back against the idea of cutting below 2%. Markets have stuck to the view that the ECB will land between 1.75% and 2% for some months now, and breaking that range would need more than one bad PMI reading. And if the weakness were to be driven by Germany, the usual suspect as of late, then the foresight of elections this Sunday may overshadow a downside surprise.

Sunday's German elections are also a reality check on market's spending expectations

The expected ramping up of military spending has pushed bond yields higher, and in the case of Bunds, the relative pricing to swaps is at its cheapest levels. The uncertainty ahead of the German elections and the implications for the constitutional debt brake were another factor pressuring Bund valuations. The outcome, and in particular the performance of smaller parties, will determine whether those in favour of - or at least open to - reforms of the brake will have the necessary votes. Looming large is also a fear that the right-wing AfD could perform better than polls suggest.

Even if a majority for reforms is obtained, we have the feeling that after all the headlines also surrounding the military spending, markets might overestimate the extent of the reform and also the timelines that might still be involved - starting with the formation of the government and then moving to the actual topic itself. Remarks from the election front-runner CDU suggest that other options of finding the financing, such as savings, should be prioritised first. The initial market reaction might be to still cheapen Bunds relatively, but we see a chance that such a move could be short-lived.

Today’s events and market view

From the eurozone, we first have French and German PMIs and then the aggregate indices. The composite index for France is expected at 48, still in contractionary territory. For Germany, consensus sees a 50.8 composite reading, dragged down by a 45.5 for manufacturing. The eurozone aggregate composite index is expected to remain close to 50. UK PMIs are also expected to remain close to 50, and for the US, a relatively stronger 53.2 is pencilled in for the composite component. From the US, we will also see the University of Michigan indices and existing home sales.
